planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun; stonecrop tolerates poor, rocky, and dry soils extremely well and rots quickly in rich, wet, heavy conditions.

directions: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun; tall stonecrop (formerly Sedum spectabile, now Hylotelephium spectabile) is a classic late-season border perennial producing bold, flat-topped flower heads of tiny, star-shaped pink to mauve flowers from late summer through fall that attract monarch butterflies, bumblebees, and native bees in extraordinary numbers. The succulent, fleshy, grey-green leaves are attractive from spring through fall, and the dried seed heads remain ornamental through winter and provide food for birds. Excellent drought tolerance once established; the main enemy is wet, poorly drained, or rich soil where it rots. Popular cultivars include 'Autumn Joy', 'Matrona', and 'Neon'.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant offsets (chicks) in spring or fall by pressing them onto the surface of very well-drained, sandy to gravelly, lean soil in full sun; hens and chicks require virtually no soil depth and thrive in rock crevices, walls, and containers with minimal growing medium.

directions: Plant offsets ('chicks') in spring or fall by pressing them onto the surface of very well-drained, sandy to gravelly, lean soil in full sun — they need almost no soil depth and anchor themselves quickly; hens and chicks are among the hardiest and most drought-tolerant succulents for cold-climate gardens, forming tight rosettes of fleshy, pointed leaves in an extraordinary range of colors from green to burgundy, purple, silver, and bicolor depending on cultivar. The 'hen' rosette blooms once (typically in year 2 or 3) with a dramatic starburst flower stalk, then dies, but leaves behind dozens of 'chicks' that fill the gap. Outstanding for rock gardens, green roofs, wall crevices, alpine troughs, dry stone walls, and containers; they thrive on negligence and rot immediately in wet, poorly drained conditions.

planting.note: Direct-sow seeds in spring in full sun in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil; little bluestem thrives in poor, rocky, clay, or sandy soils and performs poorly if over-fertilized. Container transplants establish quickly in spring or fall.

directions: Direct-sow seeds in spring or plant container transplants in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; little bluestem is the quintessential native prairie grass of the American continent and one of the finest ornamental grasses for dry, infertile sites. Blue-green to blue-silver in summer, it transforms to brilliant shades of russet, copper, and burgundy in fall, holding that color through winter while the fluffy white seed tufts shimmer in low sunlight. Outstanding for dry meadow plantings, naturalized prairie restorations, rain garden edges, and as a matrix grass for native perennial combinations. Leave standing through winter as the seed tufts are critically important to juncos, sparrows, and other seed-eating birds; cut back to 4–6 inches in early spring.

planting.note: Plant container-grown transplants or divisions in spring in full sun in very well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil; Gulf muhly is drought-tolerant once established and thrives in the heat and humidity of the Gulf Coast and Southeast.

directions: Plant container-grown transplants in spring in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Gulf muhly grass is spectacular in October and November when it erupts in an explosion of airy, rose-pink to magenta flower plumes that transform the plant into a translucent cloud of color, most dramatic in late afternoon backlighting when the plumes glow with an almost phosphorescent intensity. A clump-forming grass that stays tidy from spring through late summer when the flower display begins; it is one of the signature plants of fall Gulf Coast and Southeast landscapes. Very drought-tolerant once established and excellent for dry banks, medians, and low-water native gardens in zone 6–10. Cut back to 6 inches in late winter and divide every 3–4 years when clumps become congested.

planting.note: Direct-sow seeds in spring in full sun in any well-drained to moderately wet soil; switchgrass is highly adaptable and grows in a broader range of soil conditions than most prairie grasses — from dry upland sand to seasonally flooded clay.

directions: Direct-sow seeds in spring or plant container transplants in any well-drained to moderately wet, lean to moderately fertile soil in full sun; switchgrass is one of the most important and ecologically valuable native grasses of the North American prairie, valued for its extraordinary adaptability — it grows from Canada to Florida, from dry upland sand to seasonally flooded clay, and tolerates salt, drought, and flooding with equal resilience. The midsummer cloud of airy, panicle flowers dances above the upright, blue-green foliage, turning to golden seed heads by fall that feed dozens of native bird species. Selected cultivars offer outstanding landscape value: 'Shenandoah' turns deep red in summer, 'Prairie Sky' has intense blue foliage, and 'Northwind' is strongly upright. Cut back to 6 inches in late winter before new growth begins.

planting.note: Direct-sow seeds in fall in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic soil; cold-moist stratification over winter improves spring germination. Container transplants established in fall will bloom the following spring.

directions: Direct-sow seeds in fall or plant container transplants in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic soil; foxglove beardtongue is one of the most adaptable and deer-resistant native perennials for eastern North American gardens, producing tall, upright stems of tubular white-to-pale-lavender flowers in early summer that are one of the most productive native bee and bumblebee plants available. It thrives in the poor, dry, rocky soils of disturbed areas and prairie edges, and deteriorates in rich, moist, heavily fertilized conditions. The attractive, semi-evergreen, reddish-bronze basal foliage provides winter interest. Self-seeds moderately to maintain a naturalistic colony; popular hummingbird perennial in zone 3–8.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container transplants in spring against a very sturdy support — trumpet vine becomes extremely heavy with age and requires a substantial structure such as a fence post, large arbor, or mature tree. Full sun is essential for the best flowering.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings or container transplants against a very sturdy structure in full sun in any well-drained soil; trumpet vine is a vigorous native climber of the eastern US that attaches by aerial rootlets and can ultimately reach 20–40 feet, producing masses of large, brilliant orange-red trumpet flowers from midsummer through fall that are the single most effective hummingbird plant for zone 4–9 gardens during the critical midsummer period. Once established it is extremely drought-tolerant and essentially indestructible, but its vigor must be respected — it spreads by suckers that emerge from the roots, can become invasive in some settings, and the aerial rootlets can damage masonry and wood siding. Prune aggressively in late winter to control size; it blooms on new growth so hard pruning never reduces flowering. The cultivar 'Flava' has clear yellow flowers; 'Crimson Trumpet' is a deep red.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; silver mound artemisia absolutely requires excellent drainage and deteriorates rapidly in wet, heavy, or humus-rich soil.

directions: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; silver mound artemisia forms an impeccably neat, mound-shaped clump of finely divided, intensely silver-white, silky foliage that serves as the quintessential contrast and edging plant in the mixed perennial border, glowing with particular brightness when planted beside purple or blue flowers. It requires excellent drainage — wet, heavy, or humus-rich soil causes the mound to collapse and rot in summer. Cut back any collapsed or brown sections in late summer after heat stress; the plant resprouts vigorously from the base. Divide every 2–3 years in early spring to maintain the tight, dense mound form. An extremely useful design plant for creating visual coherence and brightness in mixed plantings.

planting.note: Direct-sow seeds in spring in full sun in very well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, neutral to slightly alkaline soil; Mexican feather grass self-seeds prolifically in suitable conditions — it is classified as invasive in California and some other western states, so check local regulations before planting.

directions: Direct-sow seeds in spring in well-drained, lean soil in full sun; Mexican feather grass forms a billowing clump of hair-fine, bright-green leaves with silky, golden-blond seed tassels from early summer onward that move in the slightest breeze — the most ethereally graceful of all ornamental grasses, giving any planting an airy, meadow-like quality. It performs best in dry, alkaline, or poor soils and in hot, sunny Mediterranean or desert-climate gardens; excessive moisture or fertility produces floppy, disease-prone plants. Self-seeds prolifically in suitable climates and is listed as invasive in California, Australia, and parts of the Southwest — consider this before planting. In zone 6–7 it may die back in severe winters but re-emerges from the roots in spring. Cut back in early spring to remove old, bleached stems.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container plants in spring or fall in full sun to partial shade in any well-drained soil; Japanese honeysuckle is adaptable to almost any site condition but is classified as invasive across much of the eastern United States — consider planting native alternatives such as coral honeysuckle (Lonicera sempervirens) instead.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings or container transplants in spring or fall in full sun to partial shade in any well-drained soil; Japanese honeysuckle is an extremely vigorous, twining evergreen vine with intensely fragrant white-to-yellow tubular flowers from late spring through summer that are one of the most recognizable scents of southern summers. However, it is classified as invasive or noxious across much of the eastern US, where it smothers native vegetation in forest understories and disturbed areas. For most planting situations, consider the native alternative coral honeysuckle (Lonicera sempervirens) which is not invasive, hummingbird-pollinated, and equally ornamental. Japanese honeysuckle is useful for erosion control on steep, disturbed slopes where invasiveness is less of a concern.
